text:
SPEECH-LANGUAGE PATHOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE
Head and Neck Oncology Program

Patient: Margaret Chen
Age: 52 years
Referring Service: Head and Neck Surgery / Radiation Oncology
Reason for Consultation: Pre-treatment swallowing evaluation prior to initiation of concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Chen is a 52-year-old East Asian woman referred for baseline swallowing assessment and dysphagia counseling prior to planned definitive concurrent cisplatin and IMRT for HPV-positive, p16-positive T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition, Stage III) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue. She presents today for pre-treatment evaluation, education, and counseling regarding anticipated treatment-related swallowing impairment.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Chen is a 52-year-old never-smoker who initially presented approximately three months ago with persistent left-sided throat discomfort and a sensation of something lodged in the back of the throat. She was treated empirically for pharyngitis by her primary care physician without resolution. Subsequent evaluation revealed a firm left level II cervical lymph node, prompting urgent ENT referral. CT of the neck demonstrated a suspicious left level IIA lymph node and subtle fullness of the left base of tongue. She was seen in ENT and underwent flexible nasopharyngoscopy, which identified an exophytic, friable lesion at the left base of tongue. Biopsy under direct laryngoscopy confirmed squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, with diffuse strong p16 overexpression and HPV-16 positivity on ISH. FNA of the left cervical node confirmed metastatic SCC, p16 positive. Staging PET-CT and MRI neck were obtained; no distant metastatic disease was identified. The primary measures 2.2 x 1.9 x 1.7 cm without extension to the vallecula or pre-epiglottic space. Single left level IIA node, 2.1 cm, with central necrosis but no radiographic extranodal extension.

The case was reviewed at multidisciplinary tumor board, and the recommendation was for definitive concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiation (IMRT, 70 Gy in 35 fractions) with high-dose cisplatin 100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks. De-escalation trial enrollment was discussed but not pursued given the PIK3CA mutation identified on tumor NGS and a decision to proceed with standard-of-care treatment. She is referred to Speech-Language Pathology prior to initiation of treatment for baseline functional assessment and pre-treatment education.

Currently, Ms. Chen reports mild left-sided throat discomfort with swallowing, most prominent with hard or dry food textures. She is eating a regular diet overall without food avoidance or weight loss at this time. She denies coughing or choking with meals, aspiration symptoms, voice changes, or nasal regurgitation. She reports no significant difficulty with thin liquids. She does note occasional mild globus sensation, which she attributes to the known primary tumor.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, T2N1M0, Stage III (AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive staging) — newly diagnosed, pre-treatment
2. No prior head and neck radiation
3. No prior surgery to the pharynx, larynx, or esophagus
4. No prior dysphagia or swallowing evaluation
5. Hypothyroidism — none documented at this time (pre-treatment)
6. No known gastroesophageal reflux disease
7. No prior neurologic conditions affecting swallowing
8. No history of aspiration pneumonia

---

SOCIAL HISTORY

Ms. Chen is a high school music teacher and lives with her husband and two adult children. She is a lifelong never-smoker with no significant alcohol use history (less than one drink per week). She is of East Asian descent and married. She remains active and working. She is motivated and engaged in understanding her upcoming treatment and its anticipated impact on swallowing and quality of life. She has a strong support system at home.

---

FAMILY HISTORY

No family history of head and neck cancer, esophageal cancer, or neurologic disease. No familial swallowing disorders noted.

---

ALLERGIES

No known drug allergies. No known food allergies.

---

MEDICATIONS

1. No standing home medications at the time of this evaluation
2. Throat lozenges as needed (over-the-counter, for throat discomfort)
3. Acetaminophen 500 mg as needed for pain

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive for: Mild left-sided odynophagia, globus sensation in the left oropharynx/throat, mild fatigue.
Negative for: Dysphagia to liquids or solid foods requiring texture modification, coughing or choking with meals, nasal regurgitation, voice change or hoarseness (notes she is a music teacher and relies on her voice professionally), unintentional weight loss, heartburn, drooling, or aspiration pneumonia. Denies any current xerostomia (pre-radiation), dysgeusia, or mouth sores. No shortness of breath.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Alert, well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Cooperative and engaged. Well-nourished.

Vital Signs: Weight 62 kg, BMI 22.1 kg/m2. ECOG performance status 1.

Oral Mechanism / Oral-Motor Examination:
- Lip closure: intact, symmetrical, no drooling
- Lingual range of motion and strength: full in all planes, no fasciculations, no atrophy
- Mandibular range of motion: trismus absent; maximal interincisal opening approximately 48 mm (within normal limits)
- Dentition: adequate; dental clearance completed prior to radiation planning
- Palatal elevation: symmetric, uvula midline; no palatal asymmetry
- Soft palate: intact movement with phonation; no velopharyngeal insufficiency observed
- Gag reflex: present bilaterally
- Tongue base retraction: mildly reduced on the left side on clinical exam, likely reflecting involvement of the left base of tongue by tumor; adequate strength on the right
- Laryngeal elevation: symmetrical and complete on clinical swallowing observation

Voice / Resonance:
- Voice quality: mildly dysphonic with slight hoarse-wet quality noted on sustained phonation "ah" — mild; likely related to involvement of left base of tongue and posterior oropharynx with the primary lesion
- Maximum phonation time: approximately 14 seconds (low-normal)
- No significant hypernasality or hyponasality

Swallowing — Clinical (Bedside) Assessment:
- Thin liquid trials (water, 5 mL x 3 and sequential sip): no overt coughing, choking, or laryngeal penetration symptoms; minimal throat-clearing after sequential sipping
- Pureed texture: swallowed without difficulty; minimal residue by clinical impression
- Regular solid (cracker): swallowed with approximately 2-3 additional swallows to clear; reports mild discomfort on the left with initiation of swallow
- No episodes of frank aspiration or wet-voice change after any texture trial

Functional Oral Intake Scale (FOIS): 7 (Total oral intake with no restrictions)

---

INSTRUMENTAL SWALLOWING EVALUATION — MODIFIED BARIUM SWALLOW STUDY (MBSS)

Study performed with fluoroscopy in cooperation with radiology. Ms. Chen was evaluated in the upright seated position. Thin liquid (Varibar Thin), nectar-thick liquid (Varibar Nectar), pureed (Varibar Pudding), and regular solid (graham cracker) trials were administered.

Oral Phase:
- Oral preparatory and transport phases intact for all textures
- Normal bolus formation and propulsion
- No anterior spillage or premature spillage into the pharynx
- Tongue base contact with posterior pharyngeal wall: mildly reduced on the left, attributed to involvement of the left base of tongue; right side compensating normally

Pharyngeal Phase:
- Velopharyngeal closure: intact
- Hyolaryngeal excursion: complete and timely
- Epiglottic inversion: adequate
- Pharyngeal stripping wave: mildly reduced amplitude on the left side, consistent with effect of the primary tumor on left tongue base mechanics
- Mild vallecular pooling with all textures, particularly thin liquids and solids (estimated 10-20% residue in left vallecula); patient able to clear with a single effortful dry swallow
- Pharyngeal constriction: mildly asymmetric, reduced on the left
- No piriform sinus stasis
- Cricopharyngeal opening: timely and adequate; no structural obstruction or dysfunction of the pharyngoesophageal segment
- No laryngeal penetration or tracheal aspiration observed with any texture or volume under fluoroscopy
- No silent aspiration identified

Esophageal screening:
- Cervical esophagus passed without obstruction; no evidence of stricture, web, or significant dysmotility on screening views

IMPRESSION OF MBSS: Mild pharyngeal phase dysphagia characterized primarily by mildly reduced left-side tongue base retraction and mild left vallecular pooling. No aspiration. No penetration. Findings are consistent with the effect of the left base of tongue primary tumor on pharyngeal mechanics. Current function is largely preserved and does not require immediate diet modification.

---

LABORATORY / NUTRITIONAL DATA

Albumin: 4.1 g/dL (within normal limits)
Pre-albumin: not obtained at this visit
Body weight: 62 kg; BMI 22.1 kg/m2
No significant nutritional deficiency identified at this time

---

IMAGING REVIEWED

MRI neck and PET-CT staging images reviewed in preparation for this consultation. Left base of tongue primary (2.2 x 1.9 cm) without involvement of the vallecula, pre-epiglottic space, or laryngeal structures. Single left level IIA node, 2.1 cm. No bilateral or contralateral disease. This anatomy informs the anticipated radiation field and expected dose distribution to swallowing structures.

---

ASSESSMENT

Ms. Chen is a 52-year-old never-smoker with newly diagnosed HPV-positive, p16-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue (T2N1M0, Stage III) presenting for pre-treatment swallowing evaluation. At baseline, she demonstrates only mild pharyngeal dysphagia, characterized by mildly reduced left-side tongue base retraction and mild vallecular pooling without aspiration, consistent with direct tumor involvement of the left base of tongue. Functional oral intake is normal with no diet restrictions required at this time. Nutritional status is adequate.

She is scheduled to begin definitive IMRT (70 Gy, 35 fractions, bilateral neck fields) with concurrent high-dose cisplatin. Based on her planned radiation fields — which include bilateral neck and oropharynx with full-dose coverage of the primary site and level II–IV cervical nodes — she is at high risk for treatment-related dysphagia. Expected acute toxicities include severe mucositis, odynophagia, and progressive impairment of tongue base retraction, pharyngeal constriction, and cricopharyngeal function secondary to radiation-induced edema and mucositis. Long-term risks include radiation fibrosis of pharyngeal constrictors, reduced epiglottic inversion, cricopharyngeal dysfunction, and xerostomia — all of which can contribute to chronic dysphagia and aspiration risk.

Given the degree of planned radiation to the pharyngeal musculature and bilateral neck, nutritional supplementation will almost certainly be required during treatment. The possibility of PEG tube placement was discussed in detail.

---

PLAN

1. Baseline Swallowing Function: Documented as described above — mild pharyngeal phase dysfunction attributable to primary tumor, no aspiration, FOIS 7. This will serve as the functional baseline for serial monitoring throughout treatment.

2. Pre-treatment Swallowing Exercises: Patient educated on and instructed to perform a prophylactic swallowing exercise program to be maintained throughout and after radiation, including:
   - Effortful swallow
   - Mendelsohn maneuver
   - Tongue base retraction exercises (tongue press against hard palate with posterior push)
   - Jaw-opening exercises for trismus prevention (Therabite device prescription placed; she was fitted and instructed today)
   - Shaker head-lift exercise (daily progressive protocol)
   The rationale for prophylactic exercises was explained: evidence suggests that maintenance of swallowing musculature activity during and after radiation may reduce the severity of long-term dysphagia and fibrosis.

3. Patient Education and Counseling: Ms. Chen was counseled extensively regarding:
   - The expected course of swallowing function during treatment: initial worsening typically begins in weeks 2-3 of CRT, with peak severity in weeks 4-6 and post-treatment period. She was reassured that most patients with HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC achieve meaningful functional recovery.
   - Mucositis management: sodium bicarbonate and saline rinses, avoidance of alcohol-based mouthwashes, magic mouthwash for comfort.
   - Importance of maintaining some oral intake (even small amounts) throughout treatment to preserve swallowing coordination — "use it or lose it" principle explained.
   - Dietary texture progression and modifications that may be helpful as mucositis develops (soft, moist foods; avoidance of acidic, spicy, or rough-textured items).
   - Signs of aspiration to report: coughing or choking with meals, recurrent fever, shortness of breath, wet or gurgly voice quality after eating.
   - Xerostomia: expected to be significant and prolonged; artificial saliva, xylitol gum, frequent hydration discussed.
   - Voice: she was counseled that temporary voice changes are expected and that her professional vocal demands as a music teacher will need to be considered in post-treatment rehabilitation planning.

4. PEG Tube Discussion: Prophylactic PEG tube placement was discussed in detail, including indications, procedure, and likely benefit during peak treatment toxicity. Ms. Chen was informed that, given bilateral neck irradiation, moderate-to-severe mucositis is anticipated and that nutritional supplementation is expected to be necessary during the treatment course. After thorough discussion of risks (tube dislodgement, infection, granuloma formation, potential for dependency) and benefits (maintained nutrition, hydration, medication delivery), the patient elected to defer prophylactic PEG tube placement at this time. She expressed understanding of the risks of significant weight loss and dehydration during treatment and agreed to a low threshold for revisiting PEG placement if oral intake becomes inadequate (defined as inability to maintain >75% estimated caloric needs orally, or weight loss >5% from baseline). She was given clear instructions on when to call for urgent re-evaluation and expedited PEG placement.

5. Nutritional Baseline Documentation: Pre-treatment weight 62 kg, BMI 22.1, albumin 4.1 g/dL. Dietary consultation co-management is recommended and referral placed; caloric goal estimated at 1800-2000 kcal/day during treatment.

6. Serial Monitoring During Treatment: SLP follow-up scheduled at approximately weeks 3, 5, and 7 of chemoradiation to assess functional status, oral intake adequacy, and need for intervention. Patient and family given direct contact information for urgent concerns.

7. Post-treatment Follow-up: Formal reassessment of swallowing function with repeat instrumental study (MBSS or FEES) planned at approximately 8-12 weeks post-completion of chemoradiation to characterize post-treatment functional status and guide rehabilitation.

Patient expressed understanding of all of the above and had her questions answered. She is appropriately counseled and motivated. She will bring her husband to her next SLP visit as requested.

Documented by: Speech-Language Pathology, Head and Neck Oncology Program
Consult discussed with referring Radiation Oncology and Head and Neck Surgery teams.
